extra.reasoning: The property G(opted_out implies not sent) is an inductive invariant preserved by every transition. The only transition that sets sent:=true is t2 (send_attempt), whose guard is `not opted_out`, so sent can only become true while opted_out is false — never producing the bad combination. The only transition that sets opted_out:=true is t1 (optout), which simultaneously sets sent:=false in the same step, so the moment opted_out becomes true, sent is false. No transition ever sets opted_out back to false, so opted_out is monotonic. While opted_out is true, every event clears sent: t1 sets sent:=false, send_attempt fires t3 (sent:=false) since t2 is disabled, and tick (t4) sets sent:=false. Thus there is no reachable state, on any execution of any length (including up to 8 events), where opted_out=true and sent=true. The initial state (opted_out=false, sent=false) satisfies the invariant vacuously. Since the base case holds and every transition preserves the invariant, the property holds for the entire reachable state space.
